title: A Single Neuron Is Sufficient to Bypass Safety Alignment in Large Language Models
gist: Apple researchers show LLM safety alignment is mediated by individual neurons that are each causally sufficient to gate refusal, so suppressing a single 'refusal neuron' bypasses safety across 7 models (1.7B-70B) with no training or prompt engineering.

insights.claim: LLM safety alignment operates through two mechanistically distinct systems: refusal neurons that gate whether harmful knowledge is expressed, and concept neurons that encode the harmful knowledge itself.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: A mechanistic map of where alignment actually lives suggests interpretability-based interventions, not just RLHF, will define the next wave of AI safety tooling.
insights.quote: safety alignment in language models operates through two mechanistically distinct systems: refusal neurons that gate whether harmful knowledge is expressed, and concept neurons that encode the harmful knowledge itself
insights.locator: Safety alignment in language models operates

insights.claim: Suppressing any one identified refusal neuron bypasses safety alignment across diverse harmful requests, and amplifying a concept neuron induces harmful content from innocent prompts.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Weight-level jailbreaks needing no prompt trickery raise the bar on open-weight model risk and could pressure regulators and enterprises toward closed/hardened deployments.
insights.quote: suppressing any one of the identified refusal neurons bypasses safety alignment across diverse harmful requests
insights.locator: Our findings suggest that safety alignment

insights.claim: The single-neuron bypass was demonstrated across seven models spanning two families and 1.7B to 70B parameters, without any training or prompt engineering.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Generality across scale and model families implies the vulnerability is structural, not a quirk, undermining confidence that current alignment is robust at any size.
insights.quote: across seven models spanning two families and 1.7B to 70B parameters, without any training or prompt engineering
insights.locator: we demonstrate both directions of failure

insights.claim: Safety alignment is not robustly distributed across model weights but is mediated by individual neurons that are each causally sufficient to gate refusal behavior.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Contradicts the assumption that alignment is diffuse and resilient, signaling that safety guarantees marketed by model vendors may be brittle.
insights.quote: safety alignment is not robustly distributed across model weights but is mediated by individual neurons that are each causally sufficient to gate refusal behavior
insights.locator: Our findings suggest that safety alignment

insights.claim: Apple's VLSU (Vision Language Safety Understanding) work argues that evaluating vision and language inputs separately misses risks where benign content becomes harmful only in joint interpretation.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Multimodal joint-risk evaluation is an emerging moat area for on-device AI safety, relevant to Apple Intelligence's image+text features.
insights.quote: missing risks from joint interpretation where benign content becomes harmful in combination
insights.locator: Safety evaluation of multimodal foundation models

insights.claim: Apple's Disentangled Safety Adapters (DSA) framework decouples safety-specific components to avoid the trade-off where guardrail models and alignment training compromise either inference efficiency or development flexibility.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Efficiency-preserving safety adapters matter directly for latency- and power-constrained on-device inference, Apple's core AI battleground.
insights.quote: often compromise either inference efficiency or development flexibility
insights.locator: Existing paradigms for ensuring AI safety
